title: Victim-blaming Kyiv, pressuring Moldova, and drone disinformation
content:
• Russia tries to shift blame for its deadly strikes on Kyiv.
• Transnistria is used as a tool to destabilise Moldova.
• Ukraine is being falsely accused of trying to drag the Baltic states into war with Russia.

Russia continued to combine military escalation with coordinated FIMI activity. Following Sunday’s overnight missile strikes on Kyiv, Russian state media falsely framed the attacks as justified retaliation while amplifying warnings of further strikes, creating psychological pressure and reinforcing narratives of Russian inevitability and escalation dominance.
snippet: • Russia tries to shift blame for its deadly strikes on Kyiv. • Transnistria is used as a tool to destabilise Moldova. • Ukraine is being falsely accused of trying to drag the Baltic states into war
creator: EU vs Disinfo
sourceDescription: DON'T BE DECEIVED, QUESTION EVEN MORE

title: The dog that didn’t bark: What the Danish election reveals about Russian influence operations
content:
According to Danish state authorities, there were no major foreign influence campaigns to speak of in the lead-up to Denmark’s March 2026 parliamentary election. Local fact-checkers and journalists tracked pro-Russian narratives and fringe propaganda channels, but nothing resembling a broad, coordinated campaign with significant reach emerged. Monitoring and analysis by Defense Innovation Highway and OpenMinds of part of the Danish online information environment during the campaign reached similar conclusions.

Given the widespread reports of Russian election interference worldwide, it may seem surprising that Denmark, a NATO member and strong supporter of Ukraine, was largely untargeted. Examining the Danish election case, we found not only national aspects that make Denmark an extraordinarily resilient example, but also broader insights into how Russian influence operations differ across geographical, political, and temporal contexts.
snippet: According to Danish state authorities, there were no major foreign influence campaigns to speak of in the lead-up to Denmark’s March 2026 parliamentary election. Local fact-checkers and journalists
creator: EU vs Disinfo
sourceDescription: DON'T BE DECEIVED, QUESTION EVEN MORE

title: Tough love: Spies, dating apps and the dark side of online intimacy
content:
Dating apps promise connection, chemistry, and maybe even love. For Ukrainian and Russian intelligence services, they also offer something else: data, emotional vulnerability, and a private channel to manipulate targets.

When Russia launched its full-scale illegal invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, the front lines extended beyond the physical battlefield into the digital space. Among many other sites, dating platforms such as Tinder and its local equivalents became operational terrain, used to recruit intelligence assets, gather information and spread propaganda.

Once a niche concern for intelligence professionals and those tracking foreign information manipulation and interference (FIMI), dating apps have become a documented channel for malicious activity. They have been used against Ukrainians and Western military personnel, and turned back against Russian forces with sometimes serious battlefield consequences.
